title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

texts.A: Mount Everest has become a high-altitude playground for the wealthy, a trend that is as dangerous as it is disrespectful. [37]The presence of 'tourists' who lack basic mountaineering skills forces Sherpas to take extreme risks to ensure their survival.[/37] This is no longer exploration; it is a commodity. [38]The environmental toll—tons of oxygen canisters and human waste—is a testament to the lack of reverence for these sacred peaks.[/38] [40]We must strictly limit permits to those who can prove a lifetime of technical climbing experience.[/40]
texts.B: The indignation over the 'crowding' of Everest often reeks of elitism. Why should the world’s highest peaks be the exclusive domain of a self-appointed climbing elite? [39]The commercialization of these peaks has brought vital infrastructure and income to impoverished mountain regions.[/39] While the environmental concerns are valid, the solution is better management of waste and logistics, not the exclusion of people who dream of standing on the roof of the world. [37]Adventure should be accessible to anyone with the funds and the will to train.[/37]
texts.C: The real tragedy of commercialized climbing is the shift in responsibility. In traditional alpinism, the climber is responsible for their own life; in the modern guided model, the guide is expected to be a guarantor of safety. This creates a dangerous psychological 'safety net' that encourages reckless behavior in thin air. [39]I agree that the local economy benefits from this industry[/39], but we must ask at what cost to the dignity of the local workers who are treated as pack animals. A mandatory code of ethics for international guiding companies is long overdue.
texts.D: Focusing on the skill level of the climbers is a distraction from the larger issue: the sheer volume of traffic. Even the most skilled climbers contribute to the degradation of the mountain when they are forced to wait in 'traffic jams' at 8,000 meters. [38]I share the view that the environmental impact is a disgrace[/38], but I disagree that more regulation is the answer. [40]Governments in these regions are too dependent on permit fees to ever enforce strict limits.[/40] The only real change will come from a cultural shift within the climbing community itself toward 'leave no trace' ethics.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on who should be allowed to climb major peaks?
questions.explanation: Expert B believes adventure should be 'accessible to anyone' with funds. Expert A insists permits should be strictly limited to those with 'technical climbing experience.'

questions.question: Which expert shares A's opinion about the environmental impact of modern climbing?
questions.explanation: Expert A highlights the 'tons of oxygen canisters and human waste.' Expert D also describes the environmental impact as a 'disgrace' and a major issue.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to C regarding the economic impact on local communities?
questions.explanation: Expert C acknowledges that the 'local economy benefits.' Expert B also states that commercialization has brought 'vital infrastructure and income' to these regions.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from D on the effectiveness of government regulation?
questions.explanation: Expert D believes governments won't enforce limits because they depend on fees. Expert A, however, actively calls for the enforcement of 'strictly limited' permits, implying it is a viable path.
